name: Sonny Greer
abstract: William Alexander "Sonny" Greer (December 13, c. 1895 – March 23, 1982) was an American jazz drummer and vocalist, best known for his work with Duke Ellington.

concerts.label: Newport Jazz Festival: Duke Ellington and His Orchestra and the Terumasa Hino Sextet
The New York Jazz Repertory Company: Duke Ellington 75th Birthday Salute

works.t: Black, Brown and Beige: Come Sunday
I Got It Bad (and That Ain't Good)

quotes.text: We brought him from Europe. First time ever in America, played at Carnegie Hall. The gypsy, from the French Hot Five. --man, that cat and guitars, make it talk. Nobody played like -- only had three fingers on one of his hands.
quotes.caption: Greer recalls Django Reinhardt's US debut at Carnegie Hall

quotes.text: Didn't mean a thing. Packed and jammed, hanging on the ceiling, chandeliers, you know. We was packed. Because when the concert -- the blizzard didn't start until after the concert started. Nobody thought -- like that was one of the big blizzards in New York. That's right. We laid that on them. We laid that on Carnegie Hall. And every year after that, the man say, you ought to come back, every year we'd come back. Come back with a different program. We would play -- Benny Goodman played, well, anytime he can play, he's going to play the same tunes. But everytime -- that's why the man liked it. We had a complete program, new. It wasn't something people heard before, and they knew that. They looked for that. Never disappointed.
quotes.caption: Greer recalls a packed Carnegie Hall concert during a blizzard

quotes.text: Not -- we played in concerts. That was written strictly for concerts. You see, Duke wrote different numbers, every concert we played at Carnegie Hall, every year
quotes.caption: Greer recalls Duke's annual Carnegie Hall concert compositions

quotes.text: Yeah, well, you see, our manager, Irvin Mills, and Duke, insisted that we look good. We were the most dressed up band in the world. We did four or five shows a day, sometimes six, at a theater, each show we changed complete, from head to toe, uniforms. Everytime we played Carnegie Hall or important on that status (gible), He would have the tailor make us complete uniforms, complete shoes, everything. Because he always -- Irving Mills was a very shrewd man. And he knew appearance was half the battle. When you look good, and can play good, you can't miss.
quotes.caption: Greer recalls Duke Ellington's emphasis on appearance and uniforms

quotes.text: Yeah, we laid it on Carnegie Hall. We laid it smack, dab on them, man, killed them.
quotes.caption: Vague recollection of a successful performance

about_quotes.speaker: Lawrence Brown
about_quotes.evidence: when you joined the band in 1932, the drummer was Sonny Greer and (he) remained the drummer all the time until you left in 1951.

about_quotes.speaker: Percy Heath
about_quotes.evidence: No, it was with Johnny Hodges and Sonny Greer. Boy, we had a good time on that gig. That was the only time I played with the Duke Ellington guys.
about_quotes.doc_title: Percy Heath — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Jones
about_quotes.evidence: Then another time I did some things with playing piano with Johnny and Lawrence, with Aaron Bell on bass and Sonny Greer on drums.

about_quotes.speaker: Loren Schoenberg
about_quotes.evidence: Drummers like Percy Brice and, and Sonny Greer. I got to work with Sonny Greer there.
about_quotes.doc_title: Loren Schoenberg interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 14, 2019

about_quotes.speaker: Charlie Holmes
about_quotes.evidence: That's one thing I used to like about Duke's band, when Sonny Greer was in the band.

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: When I joined Duke Ellington in the Cotton Club... on the drums was Sonny Greer
